## [06:01 - 09:01]

Okay, cool. You're here all good. So WordStream has like a billion dollars worth of paid traffic data spent through it every year, and they come up with benchmarks for what click-through rate or cost per lead look like in these different verticals. So if you don't have any information about what to expect for the cost for getting someone to sign it for a webinar, a good place to start is to go look at WordStream's data for the last year and find your specific industry. So if you're a lawyer, it's gonna be more expensive. I think it's something like an average of $16 a lead for them. And if you're in e-commerce, it's gonna be cheaper. It's gonna be an average of like $4 a lead, but that will give you a good starting point. And then as you start to drive traffic and think about this, you'll be able to adjust your expected cost per acquisition based on what you've seen. And looking at funnel performance from your site as well, what you see typically when someone opts in for their rate to convert to a buyer, you can factor that in from other funnels to give you your starting benchmark. Again, depending on the brand, especially if it's more of a personality, I like to tend to lean more towards native. ## [09:01 - 12:02]

when I was talking about the free video series at the top end of the funnel and creating those content delivery videos, I do feel that those should be as high end as you are able to produce. So I'm not saying that you should wait until you can afford like a video person or a team to come set up and do it. Like I definitely don't think you should wait for that. But if you do have the budget for that, I would recommend it just because once someone opts in and they start to consume the content, I do believe there's a trust factor there or this person is invested in this or they're qualified enough to be able to like produce this video quality at this level that this means something to me as the consumer. But again, don't wait for that. If you need to record it on your laptop through Zoom, that's totally fine if that's what you're doing to get proof of concept. But I think when you are producing videos as deliverables, whether they're lead magnets or video series, the quality of them, and this goes for every part of your lead magnet, whether it's a PDF or whether it's a video, I do believe that investing in the quality of that makes a big difference because it is the first impression that the consumer is having with you and they will have a higher perceived value of that information because it's presented in that way versus the reason that I tend to go a bit more native with ads is because I'm really working with Facebook's algorithm and what they're rewarding, which is a lot of what we're gonna talk about Thursday and more native or personal style videos tend to generate more social proof because they don't look like an ad and that helps us as advertisers. So it very much depends on what you're doing, but that's a great question. Yeah, and that's also a great answer. So next one, and we've got two more and then we'll get into the Facebook stuff, but someone said, is giving a coupon to get people to come to my store like 20% off a traffic system? Good question. So I mean, it definitely wouldn't be a traffic system because a traffic system is multiple steps of a customer journey, like multiple campaigns working together to move someone from completely unaware of your business to a repeat customer if that's something that you have in your business. So a coupon, that could be the offer for one of the campaigns that is a step in your traffic system. I would say that coupons are mostly used in the retargeting stage to help people overcome a barrier to price, if for some reason, the price is the reason that they're not buying. I rarely see it work, especially if you're using it in exchange for an email address at the top of Funnel to cold paid traffic, just because I don't believe

## [12:02 - 15:04]

it's strong enough of an offer unless you're e-commerce and unless you're really choosing that butter chicken funnel that as recovered on Friday and you're driving right to product page and you're wanting to get proof of concept that you can drive paid traffic to your product and convert, which is really only for e-commerce. That is when giving a coupon could help, but I don't even recommend necessarily going to a squeeze page in that scenario to ask for an email address to get that coupon code to then have them go use that coupon code unless of course you're selling through like Amazon and you're not wanting to drive directly to the Amazon listing. The reason for that is that because your goal is proof of concept in terms of generating purchases for physical traffic, again, e-commerce, I'm just gonna keep saying that, so there's not confusion. If that is your goal, we don't wanna stick something like 20% off squeeze page in front of that because that's not really what you're wanting and you don't need to generate that lead to build any sort of trust right off the bat because you are e-commerce and we talked a lot about this at the beginning of my session last week. So if you are gonna use the 20% off and you are e-commerce and you wanna use it as your offer to cold traffic, you can promote it and you'll see me do this a lot.
